titleStmt.title: Memorandum From the Executive Secretary of the Department of State (Tarnoff) to the President’s Assistant for National Security Affairs (Brzezinski)

sourceDesc.bibl: Foreign Relations of the United States, 1977–1980, Volume XVII, Part 3, North Africa, Document 126

Memorandum From
the Executive Secretary of the Department of State (Tarnoff) to
the President’s Assistant for National Security Affairs (Brzezinski)

summary.bibl: Carter Library, National Security Affairs, Brzezinski Material, Country File, Box 48, Libya: 1/77–1/81.
summary.seg: ZB 3/28/80” in the upper right-hand corner.
Sent to Brzezinski under a March 17 covering memorandum from Sick, who recommended concurrence with the Department’s proposal.
Brzezinski wrote on the note: “ok with me!”

listPerson.head: List of Persons (extract showing only persons mentioned in the present document, as described in the source volume's List of Persons)

person.occupation: Special Assistant to the Secretary and Executive Secretary, Department of State, from April 4, 1977, until February 8, 1981
Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs
member, National Security Council Staff for Middle East and North African Affairs
Secretary of State from January 23, 1977, until April 28, 1980
Chargé d’Affaires to the U.S. Embassy in Libya from August 1978 until February 8, 1980

body.head: 126. Memorandum From the Executive Secretary of the Department of State (Tarnoff) to the President’s Assistant for National Security Affairs (Brzezinski)Source: Carter Library, National Security Affairs, Brzezinski Material, Country File, Box 48, Libya: 1/77–1/81. Secret. Brzezinski wrote: “OK. ZB 3/28/80” in the upper right-hand corner. Sent to Brzezinski under a March 17 covering memorandum from Sick, who recommended concurrence with the Department’s proposal. Brzezinski wrote on the note: “ok with me!”
body.p: After the Gafsa raidSee Document 204. and the February 4 attack on the French Embassy in TripoliIn a February 4 memorandum to Carter, Vance wrote: “Both the French Embassy in Tripoli and their Consulate in Benghazi were sacked by Libyan mobs today in actions clearly related to French support for Tunisia.” (Carter Library, National Security Affairs, Brzezinski Material, Subject File, Box 22, Evening Reports (State): 2/80) we withdrew our Charge and reduced our Embassy staff to two. We now plan to have Charge William Eagleton return to Tripoli on or about March 13 for the purpose of packing up and paying farewell calls on American community leaders, his diplomatic colleagues and appropriate Libyan Government officials.

In the course of his farewell calls on Libyan officials Charge Eagleton will make it clear to them that he is departing permanently and will, in addition, use the talking points at Tab A to explain to them the reasons for this action. Brzezinski underlined “Charge Eagleton will make” and “he is departing permanently,” and highlighted this sentence. Tab A, an undated paper entitled “Suggested Talking Points for Eagleton’s Use with the Libyans,” is attached but not printed. He will inform the Libyans that we have no wish to slam the door but neither can we maintain a fully functioning Embassy under present circumstances.

There is no plan at present to replace Charge Eagleton with a senior level officer. Brzezinski underlined this sentence and highlighted the paragraph. Nonetheless we plan to maintain a small relatively junior staff (5) in Tripoli which can provide basic services to the American community and give us some political and economic reporting.

Meanwhile, we are planning to talk with the Libyan Peoples’ Bureau representative here in Washington about the status of their operations in the United States. While we have no intention of asking them to close down, we plan to insist that they bring their mission
into conformity with accepted Vienna Convention norms as outlined in our October 10, 1979 note to the Libyan Government (attached).Attached; printed as Document 107.
